body_html:
the patron has no boundaries

--- Cecilia Steen cecilia.steen@gmail.com wrote:

hi c.. please pass the below on to JEE.. thanks,

hi j... i really like this guy! this promises to be
fun... see you on
13 march, if not before.. (place to be determined).

s.

X-Sender: ruvkun@amber.mgh.harvard.edu
Date: Mon, 27 Feb 2006 16:30:03 -0500
To: g1m1c1@receptor.med.harvard.edu,
"Stephen M. Kosslyn"
smkosslyn@wjh.harvard.edu
From: Gary Ruvkun ruvkun@molbio.mgh.harvard.edu
Subject: Re: Interdisciplinary Dinner #2 Mar 13
X-Virus-Scanned: by amavisd-new at
amber.mgh.harvard.edu
Status:

george and steve
count me in. i shall again try to drive home the
point about the
pleasure genome initiative. but let me know if
this subject is too
strange for our patron.

i still think that a broad based description of
pleasure signatures
in the brain would be the farthest reaching harvard
goal, capturing
folks from economics and law and psychology and
biology. it is
also just as much about obesity as it is about
sexuality. an
exploration of fear also seems pretty timely, and
likely to reveal
just as ancient genetic precedents. and if you
explain our urges
to find mates and food, and flee from predation and
nasty
environments, there is not much else to explain.
(ie intellectual
satisfaction must be related to these more
primitive
satisfactions----why invent a new form, when just a
bit of circuit
duplication and voila, consciousness).

i also REALLY like undervaluing the cortex,
which arrogant
neurobiologists think is the only tissue worthy of
their
ruminations. it is our urges which make us pursue
goals, both short
and long term. understanding the animal parts of
the brain is a
hugely interesting goal.

more to follow on mar 13. but i promise. will
not harp on
this for more than 5 min
i will think about who else would enjoy this.....
see you
gary

--
Gary Ruvkun, Department of Molecular Biology,
Massachusetts General
Hospital
